---
title: "Enzyme- and temperature-constrained modelling of thermal performance curves in *E. coli* (eciML1515): sensitivity and identifiability"
date: today
abstract: |
  We use an enzyme- and temperature-constrained genome-scale model of
  *Escherichia coli* (eciML1515) to predict thermal performance curves (TPCs) of
  growth and to ask what generates their shape and variation. Each enzyme's
  turnover is given a macromolecular-rate-theory (MMRT) temperature dependence and
  the proteome is bounded by a shared allocation pool, so the emergent TPC is a
  balance between the thermal envelope of catalysis and the allocation of a finite
  proteome. Through a global sensitivity sweep, a variance decomposition into
  allocation and envelope contributions, a per-enzyme thermal-control and
  identifiability analysis, a DLTKcat-calibrated envelope, and a coarse-grained
  proteome-sector allocation model, we characterise the determinants of TPC shape
  and move from a nominal parameter scan to calibrated uncertainty. *(Placeholder
  abstract — to be expanded by the author.)*
---

## 1. Objective and model summary

We model the growth thermal performance curve (TPC) of *E. coli* with an
enzyme-constrained genome-scale model (eciML1515, a GECKO formulation
[@Sanchez2017]). Each enzyme carries a temperature-dependent turnover number
$k_\text{cat}(T)$ following macromolecular rate theory (MMRT), in which a negative
change in heat capacity of activation $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$ produces a curved,
optimum-bearing Arrhenius plot and hence a unimodal rate response [@Hobbs2013].
Enzyme demand is bounded by a shared proteome pool, so the predicted growth rate at
each temperature is a proteome-allocation optimum given the thermal envelope of
catalysis. TPC descriptors (optimum temperature $T_\text{opt}$, maximum rate
$r_\text{max}$, critical temperatures $CT_\text{min}/CT_\text{max}$, thermal
breadth, and an apparent activation energy $E_a$) are read from the peak-normalised
curve. This mechanistic decomposition of a TPC into an enzyme thermal envelope and
a proteome-allocation budget follows the temperature-constrained GEM programme
[@Li2021; @Pettersen2023].

The MMRT curvature parameter $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$ sets both the thermal breadth and
the apparent activation energy of the rising limb; here it is **calibrated** so the
nominal rising-limb $E_a$ matches a target value (0.65 eV, the conserved
metabolic-theory value; configurable to a measured bacterial growth-TPC $E_a$) via
bisection on $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$, rather than being hand-set for curve width. The
temperature grid is extended accordingly so $CT_\text{max}$ stays resolved.

*(Placeholder — expand with the specific pool calibration, peak-normalisation, and
descriptor definitions.)*

## 2. Nominal TPC on eciML1515

The nominal (unperturbed) TPC is shown in @fig-tpc, with the sampled ensemble fan
around it. The nominal descriptors are summarised in @tbl-nominal.

![**Nominal TPC ensemble for eciML1515.** Grey lines are individual sampled curves across proteome allocation and $k_\text{cat}(T)$ perturbations; the blue band is the interquartile range and the solid line the ensemble median, with the nominal curve dashed.](assets/figures/tpc_ensemble.png){#fig-tpc fig-pos="htbp" width=80%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-nominal
#| tbl-cap: "Nominal TPC descriptors for eciML1515 read from the sweep summary (optimum temperature, maximum growth rate, critical temperatures, thermal breadth and apparent activation energy)."
#| output: asis
import json
import pandas as pd

with open("assets/tables/summary.json") as fh:
    nom = json.load(fh)["nominal"]
rows = [
    ("T_opt (°C)",         nom["Topt_C"]),
    ("r_max (1/h)",        nom["rmax"]),
    ("CT_min (°C)",        nom["CTmin_C"]),
    ("CT_max (°C)",        nom["CTmax_C"]),
    ("Niche width (°C)",   nom["niche_width_C"]),
    ("E_a (eV)",           nom["Ea_eV"]),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["Descriptor", "Nominal"])
df["Nominal"] = df["Nominal"].round(3)
print(df.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## 3. What generates TPC variation (global sensitivity)

A Latin-hypercube sweep over the perturbation inputs (envelope shifts $dT_\text{opt}$
and spread, curvature $\Delta C_p$ scaling, and proteome-allocation knobs) yields
descriptor distributions (@fig-descdist) and Spearman sensitivity indices
(@fig-sens, @tbl-sens). The pattern distinguishes envelope-dominated descriptors
(H1.1) from allocation-dominated descriptors (H1.2): $T_\text{opt}$ and the critical
temperatures track the thermal envelope, whereas $r_\text{max}$ tracks the proteome
budget.

![**Global sensitivity of TPC descriptors.** Spearman rank-correlation between each swept input (rows) and each TPC descriptor (columns); red is positive, blue negative.](assets/figures/sensitivity_heatmap.png){#fig-sens fig-pos="htbp" width=90%}

![**Descriptor distributions across the sweep.** Marginal distribution of each TPC descriptor over the Latin-hypercube ensemble.](assets/figures/descriptor_distributions.png){#fig-descdist fig-pos="htbp" width=90%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sens
#| tbl-cap: "Spearman sensitivity indices (input × TPC descriptor) for the headline descriptors from the global sweep."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

s = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/sensitivity_spearman.csv", index_col=0)
keep = [c for c in ["Topt_C", "rmax", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if c in s.columns]
print(s[keep].round(2).to_markdown())
```

## 4. Allocation vs envelope decomposition (H1.3)

We partition the variance of each descriptor into an allocation contribution, an
envelope contribution and their interaction, using a Shapley split (@fig-decompvar,
@fig-decompshap, @tbl-decomp). @fig-decompach shows the achievable descriptor ranges
spanned by allocation alone versus envelope alone. Here the **allocation axis is the
mechanistic proteome-sector split** ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$; Basan/Scott
[@Basan2015; @Scott2010]) rather than a single scalar budget, so the H1.3
separability rests on real allocation degrees of freedom rather than a
by-construction scalar axis.

![**Achievable descriptor ranges.** Ranges of each TPC descriptor reachable by varying allocation alone versus the thermal envelope alone.](assets/figures/decomp_achievable.png){#fig-decompach fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

![**Variance partition.** Variance of each descriptor attributed to allocation, envelope and their interaction.](assets/figures/decomp_variance.png){#fig-decompvar fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

![**Shapley effects.** Shapley-normalised allocation and envelope contributions to descriptor variance.](assets/figures/decomp_shapley.png){#fig-decompshap fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-decomp
#| tbl-cap: "Variance of each TPC descriptor partitioned into allocation (A), envelope (E) and interaction (AE) components, with Shapley-normalised allocation/envelope fractions (φ_A, φ_E), from the H1.3 decomposition."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

d = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/decomposition_table.csv")
cols = [c for c in ["descriptor", "V_A", "V_E", "V_AE", "phi_A", "phi_E"] if c in d.columns]
print(d[cols].round(3).to_markdown(index=False))
```

## 5. Per-enzyme thermal control and identifiability

We compute per-enzyme thermal-control coefficients (how strongly each enzyme's
thermal parameters move each TPC descriptor) and rank enzymes by a thermal-screen
score (@fig-ctrlthermal, @tbl-control). @fig-ctrlbottle shows how the rate-limiting
enzyme changes with temperature, and @fig-ctrlident and @tbl-ident summarise how identifiable each
per-enzyme parameter is from the growth TPC alone. Few enzymes dominate thermal
control (H1.1); many thermal parameters are only weakly identifiable from growth,
motivating omics constraints (H1.2).

![**Per-enzyme thermal control.** Top enzymes ranked by their thermal-screen control score over the TPC.](assets/figures/control_thermal.png){#fig-ctrlthermal fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

![**Bottleneck enzyme vs temperature.** Identity/strength of the rate-limiting enzyme as a function of temperature.](assets/figures/control_bottleneck.png){#fig-ctrlbottle fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

![**Identifiability of per-enzyme thermal parameters.** Distribution of identifiability of per-enzyme parameters from the growth TPC alone.](assets/figures/control_identifiability.png){#fig-ctrlident fig-pos="htbp" width=75%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-control
#| tbl-cap: "Top enzymes by thermal-screen control score, with their reaction and enzyme identifiers."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

c = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/thermal_control.csv")
cols = [x for x in ["rank", "rxn_id", "enzyme_id", "thermal_screen"] if x in c.columns]
print(c[cols].head(10).round(4).to_markdown(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ident
#| tbl-cap: "Identifiability of per-enzyme thermal/catalytic parameters from the growth TPC, computed proteome-wide (all enzymes × {Topt_i, dCp_i, kcat_i}) as a first-order control-magnitude proxy. The proteome-wide identifiable fraction is small, whereas among the top-K finite-difference control enzymes (`refined`) most parameters are identifiable — few enzymes dominate control (H1.1) while most parameters need omics constraints (H1.2)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

idf = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/identifiability.csv")
n = len(idf)
n_ident = int(idf["identifiable_from_growth"].sum())
ref = idf[idf["refined"]] if "refined" in idf.columns else idf.iloc[0:0]
n_ref = len(ref)
n_ident_ref = int(ref["identifiable_from_growth"].sum()) if n_ref else 0
frac = f"{n_ident}/{n} ({n_ident/n:.1%})" if n else "n/a"
frac_topk = f"{n_ident_ref}/{n_ref} ({n_ident_ref/n_ref:.1%})" if n_ref else "n/a"
summ = pd.DataFrame({
    "quantity": ["parameters (enzymes × 3)",
                 "identifiable from growth (proteome-wide)",
                 "identifiable among top-K control enzymes",
                 "mean identifiability score"],
    "value": [n, frac, frac_topk, round(idf["ident"].mean(), 3)],
})
print(summ.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## 6. DLTKcat-calibrated envelope

Replacing the hand-set thermal envelope with per-enzyme MMRT parameters fitted from
DLTKcat $k_\text{cat}(T)$ predictions [@Qiu2024] gives the ensemble in
@fig-dltkcat. This is a data-driven envelope built from sequence/temperature-aware
turnover predictions, to be compared against the default-parameter run of §2.

![**DLTKcat-calibrated TPC ensemble.** TPC ensemble using per-enzyme MMRT parameters fitted from DLTKcat temperature-dependent $k_\text{cat}$ predictions.](assets/figures/dltkcat_ensemble.png){#fig-dltkcat fig-pos="htbp" width=80%}

## 7. Calibrated thermal-envelope uncertainty (M1.2)

We move from a nominal scan to calibrated uncertainty by sampling the thermal
envelope per enzyme with a one-factor correlation structure (shared fraction $\rho$),
optionally drawing each enzyme's mean and standard deviation from the DLTKcat fits
(`mode: posterior`). @fig-calens shows the calibrated-uncertainty fan;
@fig-calvsdef overlays the nominal hand-set ranges against this calibrated
uncertainty for the key descriptors; @tbl-calibrated gives the calibrated descriptor
medians and interquartile ranges.

![**Calibrated thermal-envelope uncertainty.** TPC ensemble under per-enzyme correlated (one-factor $\rho$) thermal sampling — the calibrated-uncertainty fan.](assets/figures/calibrated_ensemble.png){#fig-calens fig-pos="htbp" width=80%}

![**Nominal vs calibrated descriptor uncertainty.** For each key descriptor, the default hand-set-range distribution (grey) is overlaid with the calibrated per-enzyme correlated distribution (orange).](assets/figures/calibrated_vs_default.png){#fig-calvsdef fig-pos="htbp" width=85%}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-calibrated
#| tbl-cap: "Calibrated-run descriptor median and interquartile range (per-enzyme correlated envelope sampling)."
#| output: asis
import pandas as pd

cal = pd.read_csv("assets/tables/calibrated_descriptors.csv")
keep = [c for c in ["Topt_C", "rmax", "CTmax_C", "niche_width_C", "Ea_eV"] if c in cal.columns]
q = cal[keep].quantile([0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
out = pd.DataFrame({
    "Descriptor": keep,
    "Median": q.loc[0.5].values,
    "IQR": (q.loc[0.75] - q.loc[0.25]).values,
}).round(3)
print(out.to_markdown(index=False))
```

The envelope is sampled with shared fraction $\rho = 0.7$ (a one-factor
whole-proteome correlation); under `mode: posterior` the per-enzyme mean and
standard deviation are drawn from the DLTKcat fits (`strains/eciML1515/dltkcat/fits.csv`).

## 8. Proteome-sector allocation trade-off

Refining the single proteome pool into coarse-grained metabolic, biosynthesis and
maintenance sectors (a Basan/Scott growth-law partition [@Basan2015; @Scott2010])
makes the baseline rate an explicit allocation trade-off with an interior optimum:
too little metabolic pool starves enzymes, too little biosynthesis caps translation.
@fig-sectrade shows $r_\text{max}$ (and $CT_\text{max}$) against the metabolic
fraction $f_\text{metab}$, coloured by the maintenance fraction $f_\text{maint}$;
@fig-secsens gives the Spearman indices over the sector inputs. This is the
mechanistic allocation axis that speaks WP2's maintenance→biosynthesis language.

![**Proteome-sector trade-off.** $r_\text{max}$ and $CT_\text{max}$ versus the metabolic sector fraction $f_\text{metab}$, coloured by maintenance fraction $f_\text{maint}$; the interior $r_\text{max}$ optimum reflects metabolic vs biosynthesis co-limitation.](assets/figures/sector_tradeoff.png){#fig-sectrade fig-pos="htbp" width=90%}

![**Sector-sweep sensitivity.** Spearman indices of TPC descriptors against the sector inputs ($f_\text{metab}$, $f_\text{maint}$, $dT_\text{opt}$, $\Delta C_p$ scale).](assets/figures/sectors_sensitivity.png){#fig-secsens fig-pos="htbp" width=90%}

## 9. Interpretation and caveats

Across the analyses, the thermal envelope sets the position-related descriptors
($T_\text{opt}$, $CT_\text{max}$, breadth; H1.1) while proteome allocation sets the
height ($r_\text{max}$; H1.2), and the H1.3 decomposition quantifies their relative
contributions and interaction. Thermal control is concentrated in a small set of
enzymes (H1.1), yet many per-enzyme thermal parameters are only weakly identifiable
from growth alone, motivating omics constraints (H1.2).

These results are structural / in-silico predictions and carry several caveats: the
nominal $E_a$ is calibrated to a target by choosing $\Delta C_p^\ddagger$, which
simultaneously sets thermal breadth, so a low target $E_a$ implies a broad TPC (and
a high $CT_\text{max}$); the variance fractions depend on the input ranges swept; the
DLTKcat envelope inherits its predictor's accuracy (log-scale $R^2 \approx 0.6$); the
one-factor correlation $\rho$ is a simple stand-in for the true thermostability
covariance across enzymes; and the proteome-sector couplings are order-of-magnitude,
auto-calibrated relationships rather than measured parameters [@Machado2021;
@Heckmann2020; @Davidi2016].

*(Placeholder — expand interpretation and caveats.)*
